skill.name: policy-opa
skill.description: Create, test, and integrate Open Policy Agent policies for Kubernetes, infrastructure-as-code, and compliance controls.
skill.summary: Build OPA Rego policies for security, compliance, Kubernetes admission control, and CI validation.
skill.author: AgentSecOps

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-severity patterns, but review found no prompt injection, malware behavior, or credential exfiltration. Most hits are false positives from Rego policy examples, compliance terms, public reference URLs, and defensive security vocabulary. The skill is medium risk because CI examples run shell commands and download OPA over the network, which users should review before copying into pipelines.

medium_findings.title: CI Examples Execute Shell Commands and External Tools
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The GitHub and GitLab CI examples use shell command substitution, Terraform, jq, curl, and OPA commands. This is legitimate for a policy-as-code skill, but copied pipelines can execute in privileged CI contexts and should be reviewed before use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The executable shell patterns are directly present in CI YAML. The commands are ordinary OPA and Terraform workflow commands, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Terraform Plan Examples May Process Sensitive Deployment Data
medium_findings.description: Verdict: NEEDS_REVIEW. The examples generate Terraform plan JSON and evaluate it with OPA. Terraform plan data can include sensitive values depending on provider behavior, so CI logs and artifacts should avoid exposing raw plan contents.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files show Terraform plan evaluation, but they do not show secrets being printed or uploaded. The sensitivity depends on the user repository and CI configuration.

low_findings.title: Rego Security Keywords Misclassified as Malicious Indicators
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Terms such as deny, control, encryption, privileged, and network appear in defensive OPA policies. They describe controls that block insecure Kubernetes, Terraform, SOC2, PCI-DSS, and GDPR configurations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context is Rego policy authoring and compliance mapping. No command-and-control behavior or malicious instructions were found.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Ruby Backtick Execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Static analysis flagged many fenced Rego and YAML examples as backtick execution. These are documentation blocks, not Ruby strings or executable code in the skill runtime.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files are Markdown references with fenced examples. Some examples can be copied by users, but they are not hidden execution paths inside the skill.

low_findings.title: Future Keyword Imports Misclassified as Certificate or Key Files
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Static analysis treated Rego import statements containing future.keywords as certificate or key references. These imports enable Rego syntax features and do not read secrets or key material.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are Rego imports, not file paths, PEM data, private keys, or certificate loaders. This is a clear lexical false positive.

low_findings.title: Public URLs and Example Network Ranges Are Benign References
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Most hardcoded URLs are public documentation links, and 0.0.0.0/0 appears as an insecure network range that policies detect. These examples do not exfiltrate data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs point to public project or standards documentation, and the IP range is part of a policy rule detecting open ingress. No outbound data transfer logic is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution in CI Logic
dangerous_patterns.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. CI snippets assign command output to shell variables using command substitution. The commands parse locally generated JSON, but CI users should keep inputs trusted and quote variables carefully.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The syntax is directly present and executes in CI. No untrusted command construction was found, which lowers severity.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Binary Download Without Checksum Verification
dangerous_patterns.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The GitLab CI example downloads the OPA binary with curl and makes it executable. The source is the official OPA download URL, but the example does not show checksum or signature verification.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The download-and-execute pattern is explicit. It appears intended for legitimate installation, but missing verification creates supply-chain risk if copied.

content.value_statement: Security teams need repeatable policy checks across Kubernetes, infrastructure, and compliance workflows. This skill provides OPA and Rego guidance, templates, and CI examples for consistent validation.
actual_capabilities: Guides Rego policy creation for Kubernetes admission control and workload security.
Provides templates for SOC2, PCI-DSS, GDPR, and Terraform security checks.
Shows GitHub Actions and GitLab CI examples for OPA tests and policy evaluation.
Explains Gatekeeper constraint templates and example constraints for cluster enforcement.
Documents common Rego patterns for deny rules, allow rules, labels, and secret detection.
Maps policy examples to compliance controls and remediation guidance.
limitations: Policies are templates and require validation against each environment before enforcement.
The referenced helper scripts are described but not present in the scanned file tree.
Compliance mappings support engineering checks but do not replace audit or legal review.
CI examples require installed tools such as OPA, Terraform, jq, yq, Docker, or kubectl.

use_cases.title: Validate Kubernetes Deployments
use_cases.description: Create OPA or Gatekeeper policies that block privileged containers, host namespaces, unsafe capabilities, and missing security context settings.

use_cases.title: Check Terraform Plans Before Apply
use_cases.description: Review Terraform plan JSON for public databases, open security groups, missing encryption, wildcard IAM permissions, and weak backup settings.

use_cases.title: Map Controls to Compliance Evidence
use_cases.description: Use SOC2, PCI-DSS, and GDPR policy examples to connect technical checks with control names, severity, and remediation messages.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic OPA Policy
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an OPA Rego policy that denies Kubernetes Pods running privileged containers. Include a short explanation and one positive test case.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Kubernetes Manifest
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Kubernetes manifest against the bundled pod security guidance. List policy violations, severity, and practical remediation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Build Terraform Security Checks
prompt_templates.prompt: Write OPA policies for this Terraform plan to detect public RDS instances, SSH open to the internet, and unencrypted S3 buckets.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Compliance Policy Set
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an OPA policy structure for SOC2 and PCI-DSS evidence collection. Include packages, control mapping, test strategy, and CI rollout guidance.

output_examples.input: A Kubernetes Pod manifest includes a privileged container and no seccomp profile.
output_examples.output: The review flags high-risk pod security violations, explains why privileged mode is unsafe, and recommends non-root execution, dropped capabilities, and a seccomp profile.

output_examples.input: A Terraform plan creates a public database and an SSH rule open to the internet.
output_examples.output: The output identifies critical infrastructure findings, maps them to remediation actions, and suggests blocking the deployment in CI until fixed.

output_examples.input: A team asks how to align OPA checks with SOC2 controls.
output_examples.output: The response proposes control-specific policy packages, audit-friendly violation messages, test coverage expectations, and evidence retention guidance.

best_practices: Test every Rego rule with allowed and denied examples before enabling enforcement.
Pin CI tool versions and verify downloaded binaries before running policy checks.
Start with audit mode, review violations, then move high-confidence policies to blocking mode.
anti_patterns: Do not copy CI examples into production pipelines without reviewing permissions and network downloads.
Do not embed secrets, tokens, or raw sensitive Terraform plan values in policies or logs.
Do not treat generic compliance templates as proof of compliance without local control validation.

faq.question: What does this skill help create?
faq.answer: It helps create OPA Rego policies, Gatekeeper constraints, compliance checks, and CI validation workflows.

faq.question: Can it enforce Kubernetes admission control?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Gatekeeper examples and Rego patterns for blocking unsafe Pods and workload settings.

faq.question: Does it include ready-to-run scripts?
faq.answer: The documentation references helper scripts, but those scripts are not present in the scanned file tree.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium-risk warning. Users should review CI commands and verify network downloads before use.

faq.question: Which compliance frameworks are covered?
faq.answer: The templates cover SOC2, PCI-DSS, GDPR, HIPAA, NIST, and ISO 27001 style control mapping.

faq.question: Does this replace a formal compliance audit?
faq.answer: No. It supports technical validation and evidence collection, but formal compliance decisions need qualified review.
